retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous transhepatic embolization has been used to stop variceal bleeding. However, its effectiveness remains questionable. It is generally reserved for patients who are not candidates for surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management -- Surgery/Other Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal transection: in rare cases of uncontrollable, exsanguinating bleeding Liver transplantation Portosystemic shunt Inpatient admission to the intensive care unit to stabilize acute bleeding and hemodynamic status, therapeutic endoscopy. Discharge criteria: bleeding cessation; hemodynamic stability and appropriate plan for treating comorbidities. Radiology

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: TIPS is a salvage procedure to stop acute variceal bleeding. However, the procedure is also associated with serious complications including encephalopathy and occlusion of the shunt within 12 months. TIPS may be a bridge to a liver transplant.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic variceal ligation should be repeated every 1 to 4 weeks until varices are eradicated. If TIPS is done, repeat endoscopy to assess rebleeding. Endoscopic screening should be done in patients with known cirrhosis every two to three years and yearly in patients with decompensated cirrhosis. Patients with liver stiffness less than 20 kPa and with platelets greater than 150,000 can avoid endoscopic screening and may follow up by annual TE and platelet count. Prognosis In cirrhosis, one-year survival is 50% for those surviving two weeks following a variceal bleed. In-hospital mortality remains high related to the severity of underlying cirrhosis, ranging from 0% in Child A to 32% in Child C disease. Prognosis in noncirrhotic portal fibrosis is better than for cirrhotics. Complications

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management -- Postendoscopy Management
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with cirrhosis and treatment of variceal bleeding with endoscopic variceal ligation, endoscopy should be repeated after discharge every 2 to 4 weeks for potential endoscopic variceal ligation until variceal obliteration. For patients who do not undergo TIPS after treatment of acute variceal hemorrhage, a nonselective beta blocker should be initiated to decrease portal pressures after vasoactive therapy is discontinued unless the patient has an absolute contraindication to nonselective beta-blocker therapy (eg, asthma, second- and third-degree atrioventricular block without a pacemaker, sick sinus syndrome, and extreme bradycardia <50 bpm).

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management -- First-Line
retrieved_docs.content: NSBB reduce portal pressure and decrease the risk of the first bleed from 25% to 15% in primary prophylaxis Carvedilol: 6.25 mg daily is more effective than NSBB (Propranolol and Nadolol) in dropping HVPG Chronic prevention of rebleeding (secondary prevention): NSBBs and EBL reduce the rate of rebleeding to a similar extent, but beta-blockers reduce mortality, whereas ligation does not.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As many as two-thirds of patients with variceal bleeding develop an infection, most commonly spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, UTI, or pneumonia. Antibiotic prophylaxis with oral norfloxacin 400 mg or IV ceftriaxone, 1 g q24h for up to a week, is indicated. With active bleeding, avoid beta-blockers, which decrease blood pressure and blunt the physiologic increase in heart rate during acute hemorrhage. [10] [11] [12] Prevent recurrence of acute bleeding: Vasoconstrictors: terlipressin, octreotide (reduce portal pressure) Endoscopic band ligation (EBL): if bleeding recurs/portal pressure measurement shows portal pressure remains greater than 12 mmHg TIPS: Second-line therapy if the above methods fail; TIPS decreases portal pressure by creating communication between hepatic vein and an intrahepatic portal vein branch.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Can identify actively bleeding varices as well as large varices and stigmata of recent bleeding Can be used to treat bleeding with esophageal band ligation (preferred to sclerotherapy); prevent rebleeding; detect gastric varices, portal hypertensive gastropathy; diagnose alternative bleeding sites Can identify and treat nonbleeding varices (protruding submucosal veins in the distal third of the esophagus) Diagnostic Procedures/Other

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Variceal band ligation is preferred to sclerotherapy for bleeding varices and for nonbleeding medium-to-large varices to decrease bleeding risk. Ligation has lower rates of rebleeding, fewer complications, more rapid cessation of bleeding and a higher rate of variceal eradication. Repeat ligation/sclerosant for rebleeding. If endoscopic treatment fails, consider self-expanding esophageal metal stents or peroral placement of Sengstaken-Blakemore-type tube up to 24 hours to stabilize the patient for TIPS.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Once a patient has a single episode of variceal bleeding, there is a 70% chance of a rebleed. At least 30% of rebleeding episodes are fatal. Most deaths occur within the first few days after the bleed.  Mortality rates are highest in the presence of surgical intervention and for acute variceal bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The first indication of varices is often the presence of a gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding episode: hematemesis, hematochezia, and/or melena. Occult bleeding (anemia) is uncommon. History Variceal bleed can be the initial presentation of previously undiagnosed cirrhosis Alcoholism, exposure to blood-borne viruses Hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia Rapid upper GI bleed can present as rectal bleeding. Weight loss in patients with chronic liver disease Anorexia Abdominal discomfort Jaundice Pruritus Encephalopathic symptoms- altered mental status Muscle cramps Physical Exam

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of Acute Gastric Variceal Bleeding The most crucial step in managing patients presenting with acute gastric variceal bleed is admitting the patient to the ICU for initial resuscitation with packed red blood cells (PRBCs) transfusion and respiratory status protection. PRBCs transfusion should be limited to a target hemoglobin of approximately 8 g/dL Associated coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia should be corrected. The patient should be maintained on empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, especially in patients with cirrhosis, to decrease the risk of bacterial infections, which is associated with increased rates of mortality. The role of vasoactive agents such as octreotide, vasopressin, terlipressin, and somatostatin in gastric variceal bleeding management is not clear. Nevertheless, they are still utilized because of their safety and potential benefits. Urgent upper endoscopy must be performed for diagnosis and treatment. Endoscopic Therapies

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management -- Standard-of-care Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Standard-of-care therapies include epinephrine injection, contact and noncontact thermal coagulation, mechanical through-the-scope and over-the-scope clips, and variceal ligation. Epinephrine injection reduces or stops bleeding through vasoconstriction and local tamponade. Epinephrine monotherapy is not advised due to its temporizing effect. Bipolar electrocoagulation is a contact thermal treatment that uses a probe to compress the vessel, coagulates directly, and seals the vessel. A meta-analysis of 15 RCTs showed that bipolar electrocoagulation reduces further rebleeding and mortality compared to no treatment. [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although gastric varices and portal hypertension an incidental acute gastric variceal bleeding is lower than esophageal variceal bleeding, gastric varices have a high propensity to bleed severely. They are often associated with higher mortality than esophageal variceal bleeding. The overall prognosis is dependent on the severity of the underlying disease and commonly used prognostic model such as Child-Pugh score and Model for end-stage liver disease (MELD) score is useful to predict the severity of underlying liver disease and also are effective in estimating the risk of mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Data regarding the role of endoscopic therapy for the treatment of bleeding gastric varices is limited. However, endoscopic hemostasis is the mainstay in the management of patients presenting with acute gastric variceal bleeding. Current available endoscopic interventions include endoscopic injection sclerotherapy, tissue adhesives such as cyanoacrylate-based tissue adhesives, thrombin, and endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL). [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Video capsule endoscopy screening may be an alternative to traditional endoscopy

retrieved_docs.content: cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 810
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is categorized as a form of upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and frequently presents with esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء). It is closely associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية), highlighting the clinical importance of identifying variceal manifestations when evaluating and managing patients with gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1176
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is classified under upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and is frequently associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية). One of the key manifestations of gastrointestinal hemorrhage is the presence of esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء), which arise in the esophageal plexus (مفاغرة مريئية). Recognizing that esophageal varices represent a specific source of bleeding within the broader category of upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage helps clinicians target diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic interventions, such as endoscopic control of variceal bleeding, to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ. Hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension predispose patients to دوالي المَريء, which can progress to نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). This bleeding is a major cause of نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage) and is linked to نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding). Overall, the framework links esophageal variceal pathology, its underlying vascular morphology, associated bleeding manifestations, alcohol‑related liver disease, portal hypertension, and the endoscopic therapeutic procedures used for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1158
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage, which is classified under نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal bleeding), commonly manifests as دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices). These varices are located in the مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal region) and represent وريد متدلي (dilated veins). نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding) is directly related to these dilated veins, indicating that bleeding from the varices is a typical presentation of gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Management of this condition is addressed by الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء (esophageal therapeutic procedures), which include ربط دوالي المريء (ligation of esophageal varices) and interventions targeting المفاغرة المريئية. The overall relationships highlight that gastrointestinal hemorrhage is associated with variceal bleeding and that esophageal varices, as dilated veins in the esophagus, are both a manifestation and a therapeutic target in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: (alcoholic cirrhosis), which belong to the SMQ encompassing الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد (liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other liver‑damage conditions). Therapeutic interventions specific to the esophagus—ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal sclerotherapy)—are employed to control نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding) and related variceal hemorrhage. Recognizing these interconnections guides clinicians to identify patients at risk due to liver disease and alcohol use, understand the development of esophageal and gastric varices, and select appropriate endoscopic treatments for upper GI bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1321
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is recognized to have a manifestation in the form of دوالي المريء (esophageal varices), which are located in the مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal region). This type of hemorrhage is also associated with نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (venous variceal bleeding), underscoring the link between variceal pathology and gastrointestinal bleeding. The مجموعة الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء (esophageal therapeutic procedures) specifically address these variceal lesions and include ربط دوالي المريء (ligation of esophageal varices) as well as مفاغرة مريئية (additional esophageal variceal interventions). Together, these connections emphasize the importance of identifying esophageal varices as a source of gastrointestinal hemorrhage and applying targeted esophageal treatments to manage and prevent variceal bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1716
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices is a therapeutic endoscopic procedure that is directly related to دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices) and reflects the indirect morphology of وريد متدلي (dilated veins). دوالي المَريء can present as gastrointestinal hemorrhage, indicating that bleeding from these dilated esophageal veins is a common manifestation of upper gastrointestinal bleeding. نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding) is classified under وريد متدلي, emphasizing that it originates from dilated venous structures, and it is closely associated with gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Together, these relationships illustrate that the endoscopic injection technique targets esophageal varices—dilated veins prone to variceal bleeding—which can lead to significant gastrointestinal hemorrhage, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing these interconnected conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhage is a child of نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal bleeding) and is associated with نزيف الدوالي الوريدية, illustrating how variceal bleeding contributes to upper GI hemorrhage. Overall, the knowledge base integrates vascular and lymphatic esophageal diseases, alcohol use, liver pathology, and targeted endoscopic therapies in the pathogenesis and management of esophageal variceal bleeding and upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Operation on esophageal varices” is defined by a morphology that involves a وريد متدلي (dilated or hanging vein). The clinical condition نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding) is described as having a وريد متدلي as its parent structure, indicating that the bleeding originates from a dilated vein. Consequently, the وريد متدلي serves as the central anatomical element linking both the therapeutic intervention for esophageal varices and the pathogenesis of variceal hemorrhage, highlighting its significance in the diagnosis and management of esophageal vascular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2749
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices is an endoscopic procedure that is directly linked to the condition دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices). Gastrointestinal hemorrhage may manifest with دوالي المَريء, indicating that bleeding within the gastrointestinal tract can arise from or be identified by the presence of esophageal varices. Therefore, patients presenting with gastrointestinal hemorrhage are often evaluated for دوالي المَريء, and endoscopic injection therapy is employed to control variceal bleeding and reduce the risk of further hemorrhagic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: upper GI therapeutic procedures classify the esophagoscopy interventions. Diagnostic imaging of the esophagus includes upper GI x‑ray, and therapeutic options within إجراءات علاجية خاصة بالمريء (esophageal therapeutic procedures) comprise ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal stenting) to manage variceal bleeding and esophageal obstruction. Integrated assessment of alcohol use, liver disease, variceal formation, and appropriate endoscopic or radiologic interventions is essential for optimal patient care in esophageal and related vascular disorders.

Overall, the knowledge base connects variceal conditions of the esophagus, broader esophageal disorders, venous and lymphatic disease classifications, alcohol‑related liver injury, and severe liver disease, illustrating how these entities interrelate in the context of diagnosis, classification, and clinical risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1143
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is a classification that includes دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف (esophageal varices without reported bleeding) and نزيف دَوالِي المريء (bleeding esophageal varices). تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic cirrhosis) is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء, indicating that patients with cirrhosis are at heightened risk for esophageal variceal bleeding. تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis) is a subtype of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, linking alcohol‑related liver disease to the broader condition of hepatic cirrhosis and its associated risk of bleeding esophageal varices. This interconnected knowledge underscores the importance of monitoring variceal status in cirrhotic patients, particularly those with an alcoholic etiology, to guide appropriate clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1169
retrieved_docs.content: Variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية) originates from dilated veins (وريد متدلي) and represents a specific cause of gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Gastrointestinal hemorrhage includes bleeding throughout the digestive tract, with upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نزيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) being a major subgroup. Consequently, variceal bleeding is a subtype of upper gastrointestinal bleeding and is closely associated with overall gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Recognizing that variceal bleeding stems from dilated veins aids clinicians in identifying the source of bleeding and optimizing management for patients experiencing gastrointestinal hemorrhage.
